skill.name: linkedin-content
skill.description: LinkedIn post writing with hook formulas, formatting rules, and engagement patterns. Covers post types, algorithm signals, character limits, and content pillars. Use for: LinkedIn posts, professional content, thought leadership, B2B content, personal branding. Triggers: linkedin post, linkedin content, linkedin writing, linkedin strategy, linkedin engagement, linkedin algorithm, linkedin hook, linkedin formatting, thought leadership, professional content, b2b content, linkedin growth
skill.summary: LinkedIn content writing skill with hook formulas, character limits, formatting rules, and algorithm optimization for high engagement.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: This skill is pure markdown documentation providing LinkedIn content writing guidance. Static scanner flagged markdown code examples as security issues (external_commands, network URLs, pipe-to-shell patterns), but these are all legitimate documentation examples showing CLI usage. No actual code execution, network data exfiltration, or malicious patterns present. All static findings are FALSE POSITIVES - documentation examples misinterpreted as executable code.

content.value_statement: This skill helps users create LinkedIn posts that capture attention and drive engagement using proven hook formulas, formatting rules, and algorithm optimization techniques.
actual_capabilities: Generate hook formulas for high-engagement openings
Apply correct formatting with line breaks for mobile readability
Optimize posts for LinkedIn algorithm signals
Identify content pillars for consistent posting strategy
Avoid common mistakes that reduce post reach
limitations: Does not directly post to LinkedIn (requires external CLI tool)
Does not provide real-time analytics or engagement data
Does not generate images directly (requires separate skills)
Content recommendations are based on general best practices, not individual account performance

use_cases.title: Personal Brand Building
use_cases.description: Professionals building their personal brand create consistent, high-value posts that establish expertise and attract opportunities.
use_cases.target_user: Founders, executives, consultants, and career-focused professionals

use_cases.title: B2B Marketing Teams
use_cases.description: Marketing teams create thought leadership content to generate leads and build brand authority in their industry.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing managers, content strategists, and B2B companies

use_cases.title: Career Development
use_cases.description: Job seekers and career changers use LinkedIn to build visibility and attract recruitment opportunities.
use_cases.target_user: Job seekers, recruiters, and career coaches

prompt_templates.title: Basic Post Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post about [TOPIC]. Use a strong hook in the first 2 lines, include 3-5 relevant hashtags, and end with a question to drive comments.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a simple professional post on a given topic

prompt_templates.title: Story-Based Post
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a personal story LinkedIn post about [EXPERIENCE]. Include the setup, the challenge, the turning point, and the lesson learned. End with an engagement question.

prompt_templates.title: Contrarian Opinion Post
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a contrarian LinkedIn post that challenges a common belief in [INDUSTRY/FIELD]. Start with a bold statement, provide evidence or examples, and invite discussion.

prompt_templates.title: Tips List Post
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a numbered list LinkedIn post with [NUMBER] tips for [TOPIC]. Each tip should be 1-2 sentences with a brief explanation. Include a compelling hook and CTA.
prompt_templates.scenario: Sharing actionable advice in list format

output_examples.input: Write a post about the importance of code reviews
output_examples.output:
Unpopular opinion: most code reviews are a waste of time.

Not because code reviews aren't valuable. But because we use them wrong.

We treat reviews as gatekeeping. Instead, they should be collaboration.

Here's what actually works:

1 in. Review batches, not continuously
2. Focus on logic, not style (let linters handle that)
3. Ask questions, not demands
4. Approve with minor comments - don't block for nitpicks

The best code review? One where both parties learn something.

What's your take - are code reviews broken? 👇

output_examples.input: Write a personal story post about a career failure
output_examples.output:
I got fired on a Tuesday.

It was my first startup. We had 3 months of runway. I was convinced we were about to figure it out.

We didn't.

But here's what I learned:

Failure is not the opposite of success. It's the path to it.

That 'failure' taught me more than 5 years of comfortable jobs ever could.

If you're going through something similar: this is not the end. It's the beginning of a better story.

What's the best thing that ever happened to you through a 'failure'?

best_practices: Focus the first 210 characters on a compelling hook - this determines if people click 'see more'
Write one sentence per line with blank lines between paragraphs for mobile readability
End every post with a question or CTA to drive comments and engagement
anti_patterns: Starting with corporate jargon like 'I am pleased to announce' or 'In today's rapidly evolving landscape'
Writing dense walls of text without line breaks - nobody reads them on mobile
Using more than 5 hashtags - looks spammy and triggers algorithm penalties

faq.question: What is the ideal LinkedIn post length?
faq.answer: The ideal length is 1300-1500 characters. This ensures the hook is visible before 'see more' while providing enough value for the algorithm to reward with reach.

faq.question: How many hashtags should I use?
faq.answer: Use 3-5 relevant hashtags. More than 5 can trigger spam filters and reduce reach. Place them at the end of the post.

faq.question: When is the best time to post on LinkedIn?
faq.answer: Tuesday through Thursday between 7-8 AM, 12 PM, and 5-6 PM in your audience's timezone. Avoid weekends for business content.

faq.question: How important is the hook in a LinkedIn post?
faq.answer: The hook is critical. The first 210 characters determine if people click 'see more'. A weak hook means zero reach regardless of how good the rest of the content is.

faq.question: Should I include links in my LinkedIn posts?
faq.answer: Avoid links in the main post - LinkedIn's algorithm reduces reach for external links. Instead, add the link as the first comment or ask people to DM for it.

faq.question: How do I build a content strategy on LinkedIn?
faq.answer: Use content pillars. Define 3-5 topics you will consistently write about. Rotate between expertise, personal stories, opinions, behind-the-scenes, and curated insights.
